meta.name: CFQ Cognitive Failures Questionnaire
meta.description: The CFQ is a 25-item self-report measure of everyday cognitive failures, including failures of perception, memory, and motor function. It assesses the frequency of common mistakes in daily life over the past six months.
meta.author: Broadbent, Cooper, FitzGerald & Parkes (1982)
meta.reference: Broadbent DE, Cooper PF, FitzGerald P, Parkes KR. The Cognitive Failures Questionnaire (CFQ) and its correlates. Br J Clin Psychol. 1982;21(1):1-16.
meta.instruction: The following questions are about minor mistakes which everyone makes from time to time, but some of which happen more often than others. We want to know how often these things have happened to you in the past six months.

questions.text: Do you read something and find you haven't been thinking about it and must read it again?
Do you find you forget why you went from one part of the house to the other?
Do you fail to notice signposts on the road?
Do you find you confuse right and left when giving directions?
Do you bump into people?
Do you find you forget whether you've turned off a light or a fire or locked the door?
Do you fail to listen to people's names when you are meeting them?
Do you say something and realize afterwards that it might be taken as insulting?
Do you fail to hear people speaking to you when you are doing something else?
Do you lose your temper and regret it?
Do you leave important letters unanswered for days?
Do you find you forget which way to turn on a road you know well but rarely use?
Do you fail to see what you want in a supermarket (although it's there)?
Do you find yourself suddenly wondering whether you've used a word correctly?
Do you have trouble making up your mind?
Do you find you forget appointments?
Do you forget where you put something like a newspaper or a book?
Do you find you accidentally throw away the thing you want and keep what you meant to throw away?
Do you daydream when you ought to be listening to something?
Do you find you forget people's names?
Do you start doing one thing at home and get distracted into doing something different?
Do you find you can't quite remember something although it's "on the tip of your tongue"?
Do you find you forget what you came to the shops to buy?
Do you drop things?
Do you find you can't think of anything to say?

ranges.label: Low Cognitive Failures
ranges.description: Your score suggests a low frequency of everyday cognitive failures. You appear to maintain good attentional control and memory in daily life.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your current level of attentiveness
Keep up healthy habits such as adequate sleep and regular exercise
Practice mindfulness to sustain cognitive sharpness

ranges.label: Below Average Cognitive Failures
ranges.description: Your score is below the population average, indicating a relatively normal frequency of minor cognitive slips.
suggestions: Consider using checklists or reminders for important tasks
Minimize multitasking when focused attention is needed
Ensure adequate sleep and manage stress levels

ranges.label: Above Average Cognitive Failures
ranges.description: Your score is above the population average, suggesting you experience cognitive failures more frequently than most people.
suggestions: Develop organizational strategies such as to-do lists and calendars
Reduce distractions in your environment when performing important tasks
Prioritize quality sleep and stress management
Consider whether fatigue, stress, or mood may be contributing factors

ranges.label: High Cognitive Failures
ranges.description: Your score indicates a high frequency of cognitive failures. This may be associated with stress, fatigue, anxiety, or attentional difficulties.
suggestions: Consider consulting a healthcare professional to rule out underlying causes
Implement strong organizational systems (reminders, alarms, checklists)
Address potential contributing factors such as sleep deprivation or chronic stress
Practice attention-training exercises or mindfulness meditation
Discuss with a professional whether further cognitive assessment may be helpful
